text: Hi it's just a short video showing what is called a robot assisted partial nephrectomy which is removal of a small tumour from the kidney without the need to remove the whole kidney
The surgeon applies a clamp to the renal artery, visibly occluding the blood flow to the kidney in preparation for the partial nephrectomy.
The surgeon selectively clamps the vessel supplying the upper pole of the kidney, preparing to remove an abnormality located at the upper pole.
The surgeon identifies and occludes the feeding blood vessel to the kidney, resulting in a bloodless surgical field during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully peels away a small tumor from the kidney, placing it aside for retrieval at the end of the procedure. The tumor separates easily within the correct surgical plane.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis by suturing the base of the kidney using a barbed V-Lock suture with a large needle. The suture features a loop at the end, allowing it to be snugged down without tying a knot, before closing the defect.

obviously saves time and time is of the essence when you're doing this kind of operation you ideally don't want the clamp on the blood vessel for more than twenty minutes I sometimes do early release of the clamp to minimize damage to the kidney this is what I like to do in this case so I remove the clamp and it will only be on for two or three minutes
The surgeon has restored blood flow to the upper pole of the kidney, which was previously selectively clamped, allowing the tissue to regain its normal coloration.
The surgical field shows minimal bleeding as the tumor is located at the very edge of the kidney, where the blood vessels are less numerous.
The surgeon runs a continuous suture up the length of the kidney base using a barbed suture, ensuring the stitch holds securely without slippage.

It's the stitch that I first used during my prostatectomies for prostate cancer, but then I found it transferred very nicely to the partial nephrectomy setting. I find it quite easy to achieve hemostasis and close the kidney using this suture.

I think the robot has really really helped with this operation more than any other operation. This type of suturing laparoscopically can be technically challenging and when time is of the essence and it's in a stressful situation then that adds further difficulties. The robot makes suturing so much more simple and straightforward because of the wristed movements of the instruments. It allows you to create different angles to allow you to place the needle in the best possible place. It also allows transfer of the needle from one needle holder to the other very easily. It makes for a swift closure of the kidney.

As I say, I think it is the biggest advance in surgery, the robots, for this operation. No longer need to use a, to have a large, potentially morbid open loin incision, which sometimes needed the, or led to the breaking of the rib.

I think you've definitely removed some of the potential complications you saw with laparoscopic partial nephrectomy, such as bleeding, urine leaks and prolonged clamping of the kidney, leading to deteriorating renal function.

This was a relatively simple, straightforward case The robot allows you to now potentially tackle more challenging tumours in terms of location within the kidney and also size. You can now potentially attempt to remove larger tumours because of the advantages of the robot.

The surgeon uses a hemlock clip to snug the stitch down, then continues to run the stitch along the kidney's exterior. The barbs on the stitch visibly hold the kidney tissue together, allowing for tension adjustment with the hemlock clip.

So operations are quite painless. You have five, one centimeter incisions in the upper part of your abdomen. You're in hospital two nights and you will be kind of back to normal activities within two to four weeks.
The surgeon places the final clip using robotic assistance to complete the partial nephrectomy procedure.
